metadata.summary: Ownership rules, borrow checking, smart pointers (Arc, Mutex, Rc, Box, Cow), and lifetimes for memory-safe Rust design.

content.title: Rust Ownership Model
content.summary: Rust's ownership system enforces memory safety without GC: each value has one owner, borrows are either multiple shared (&T) or one exclusive (&mut T), and lifetimes express reference validity across scope boundaries. Use Arc<T> for read-only sharing across threads, Arc<Mutex<T>> for shared mutable state, and Cow<'a, str> to avoid cloning when the input might not need mutation. Pass &str/&[T]/&Path in function signatures; only accept String/Vec<T> when storing.

why.p: The borrow checker is the primary correctness mechanism in Rust. Most agent-generated Rust code fails because LLMs reach for .clone() or 'static bounds to silence compiler errors rather than restructuring the ownership graph. A plan-then-implement loop — draft the ownership topology first, then code one module at a time with cargo check as the feedback signal — catches structural mistakes before they cascade.

list.item: New contributor (human or LLM) writing code that crosses function or thread boundaries with non-Copy types.
Reviewing PRs that introduce Clone, Rc, Arc, RefCell, or lifetime annotations.
Designing data flow for a long-running service (connection pool ownership, shared mutable state).
Refactoring code that compiles only because of excessive clone() calls.
Modeling builder/typestate APIs that consume self to enforce state machines.
Pure-CPU numeric code on Copy primitives — borrow checker rarely fires.
One-off scripts or examples/ where clone() cost is irrelevant.
FFI thin wrappers where lifetimes are dictated by the C API.
Throwaway prototypes — fighting the borrow checker before the design is settled wastes tokens.

section.name: ownership-rules-and-borrowing
section.p: The three ownership rules, move semantics, Copy vs Clone, and the borrow checker's borrowing constraints.

rule.statement: Each value has exactly one owner. When the owner goes out of scope, the value is dropped. Assignment moves ownership for non-Copy types; Copy types are bitwise-copied.
rule.rationale: Enforced at compile time. No GC pause, no dangling pointers. The compiler rejects use-after-move at the call site.

example.title: Move semantics and Copy

rule.statement: Multiple shared borrows (&T) are allowed simultaneously. Only one exclusive borrow (&mut T) is allowed at a time, and it cannot coexist with shared borrows.
rule.rationale: The borrow rules eliminate data races at compile time: shared = read-only, exclusive = sole writer.

example.title: Borrowing rules and scope

antipattern.description: Keeping an immutable reference live while attempting a mutable borrow of the same value.
antipattern.reason: The compiler rejects this to prevent data races. Restructure so the shared borrow ends before the mutable one begins.

rule.statement: Add explicit lifetime annotations only when the compiler cannot infer them. Do not add lifetime parameters to every type "to be safe" — it corrupts public APIs.
rule.rationale: Spurious lifetimes force every caller to annotate too. The compiler infers lifetimes in the vast majority of cases through lifetime elision rules.

example.title: Explicit lifetimes where needed

rule.statement: Pass &str, &[T], &Path in function parameters. Accept String/Vec/PathBuf only when the function stores the value. Use impl Into<String> in builder constructors for ergonomic caller API.
rule.rationale: Taking &str accepts both &String and &str. Taking String forces the caller to allocate or clone. The difference is zero-cost for the callee and visible at every call site.

section.name: smart-pointers-box-rc-arc-mutex-cow
section.p: When to use each smart pointer type and how they relate to the ownership model.

rule.statement: Default to Arc<T> for read-only sharing across threads. Use Arc<Mutex<T>> only when mutation is needed. Use Rc/RefCell only in single-threaded contexts.
rule.rationale: Arc is thread-safe. Rc is not. Confusing the two compiles fine in single-threaded code but produces a cryptic "not Send" error as soon as a thread is introduced.

list.item: Box<T> — heap allocation, single owner, zero runtime overhead. Use for recursive types (enum List) and trait objects (Box<dyn Trait>).
Rc<T> — reference-counted, single-threaded shared ownership. Use for data shared within one thread that has unclear single owner.
RefCell<T> — interior mutability, runtime borrow checking. Panics on borrow conflict at runtime instead of compile time.
Arc<T> — atomically reference-counted, thread-safe shared ownership. Use for AppState, connection pools, shared config.
Mutex<T> — mutual exclusion. Pair with Arc for shared mutable state across threads.
Cow<'a, str> — clone-on-write. Returns Borrowed when no mutation needed, Owned when it is.

example.title: Arc in shared service state

antipattern.description: Using Rc or RefCell in async code that is sent to tokio::spawn.
antipattern.reason: Rc and RefCell are not Send/Sync. tokio::spawn requires 'static + Send. The compile error is a correct diagnosis: redesign ownership with Arc.

example.title: Cow for conditional mutation

antipattern.description: Multiple overlapping borrow_mut() calls on the same RefCell across await points.
antipattern.reason: borrow_mut() panics at runtime if already borrowed. Unlike compile-time borrow errors, these surface only under specific execution paths in production.

section.name: builder-pattern-struct-ownership-closure-captures
section.p: Builder pattern consuming self for typestate, struct ownership methods, and move closures.

rule.statement: Builder methods take self (not &mut self) and return Self. This enforces at the type level that the builder cannot be used after build() is called.
rule.rationale: Consuming self prevents cloning a partially-built builder and calling build() twice with subtly different states. The typestate pattern is zero-cost.

example.title: Builder consuming self
Struct method ownership patterns

rule.statement: Use move closures when the closure outlives the current scope or is passed to tokio::spawn. Without move, the closure borrows locals which may not live long enough.
rule.rationale: tokio::spawn requires 'static. A closure that borrows a local variable cannot be 'static. move transfers ownership into the closure, making it self-contained.

example.title: Move closures for async spawning

section.name: ownership-gotchas-and-agent-failure-modes
section.p: Self-referential structs, async + borrows, clone abuse, and 'static misuse — the most common LLM-generated ownership failures.

antipattern.description: A struct field that borrows from another field of the same struct (self-referential).
antipattern.reason: Not expressible in safe Rust without Pin + unsafe or crates like ouroboros. Agents write these confidently and produce "cannot borrow as mutable" or "borrowed value does not live long enough" errors. Redesign to hold owned data or use indices instead of references.

antipattern.description: Capturing a reference to a local variable inside tokio::spawn(async move { ... &ref ... }).
antipattern.reason: tokio::spawn requires 'static. Local references are not 'static. The "fix" of adding 'static to the type is wrong — use Arc::clone to share ownership instead.

example.title: Proper shared ownership with Arc

antipattern.description: Adding .clone() to silence a borrow-checker error without understanding the lifetime conflict.
antipattern.reason: Gratuitous clones double allocation, hide architectural problems, and mask bugs that the borrow checker was correctly diagnosing. Force agents to articulate the lifetime conflict before applying any clone.

rule.statement: When a borrow-checker error appears, diagnose the conflict first. If the fix is .clone(), justify it: "this is cheap because T is a small struct" or "this value must be owned by two futures". Reject silent clone additions.
rule.rationale: Silent clone additions accumulate and become performance problems. Each one should be a conscious trade-off, not a reflexive silencer.

antipattern.description: Adding 'static bounds to structs, function parameters, or generics "to make it compile".
antipattern.reason: 'static means the type must live for the entire program. Adding it to an API parameter forces every caller to provide a leaked or globally-static value. Use Arc instead.

template.purpose: Clippy script surfacing needless clone(), to_string(), to_owned() calls in a crate.
